authoritative_sources.title: 12 CFR Part 1005, Electronic Fund Transfers (Regulation E)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Primary U.S. regulation governing electronic fund transfer error resolution, disclosures, unauthorized-transfer handling, and consumer-facing timing obligations relevant to fintech operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Transfer-servicing baseline, customer-impact checks, and completion gating

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic Fund Transfers FAQs
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official CFPB guidance clarifying how Regulation E applies to digital wallets, P2P transfers, prepaid accounts, and other fintech product structures that materially change servicing expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Fintech-specific product scoping and exception interpretation
used_for: boundary handling when product structure changes obligations

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA) Examination Procedures
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official CFPB examination manual describing the records, transaction samples, complaints, exception processes, and evidence sets examiners review for EFT servicing controls.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Evidence normalization, control review, and completion documentation

authoritative_sources.title: Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, FDIC, and OCC
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Primary interagency banking guidance for planning, due diligence, contract negotiation, ongoing monitoring, and termination of third-party relationships that define sponsor-bank and embedded-finance operating boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Program model scoping, approval-boundary definition, and partner-role escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Conducting Due Diligence on Financial Technology Companies: A Guide for Community Banks
authoritative_sources.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, FDIC, and OCC
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Joint banking-agency guidance tailored to bank-fintech relationships that identifies the operational, business, audit, compliance, and information-security records banks use to evaluate fintech programs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Operational record inventory and dependency definition
used_for: failure mode definition around missing program context

authoritative_sources.title: Reversals and Enforcement
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Nacha is the recognized ACH Network rulemaking body. Its public guidance defines reversal classes, timing boundaries, unauthorized-debit documentation expectations, and exception terminology used in ACH operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ACH exception handling, rework, and blocked-state logic

authoritative_sources.title: PCI DSS v4.0.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Recognized payment-card control standard establishing logging, access, data-handling, and evidence expectations for card-related operations artifacts handled by fintech servicing teams.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Sensitive evidence handling and audit-ready completion packaging
used_for: control baseline for card-related evidence handling

workflow_stages.name: Accepted intake and fintech-program qualification
workflow_stages.description: Identify the product, rail, sponsor-bank or licensed-entity structure, program-manager role, and controlling systems before any operational claim is made.

workflow_stages.name: Source-of-truth retrieval and evidence normalization
workflow_stages.description: Pull the ledger, processor, settlement, customer-communication, and approval records needed to establish whether the queued work item can move safely.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Execution progression and approval-aware fulfillment
workflow_stages.description: Move work through accepted, in-progress, blocked, rework, ready-for-completion, and completed states only when the source records and documented owner approvals support the next step.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception queue and adjacent-authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when ACH or processor exceptions, consumer-impact concerns, sponsor-bank controls, complaint or Reg E interpretation, or suspicious-activity signals leave the lane's operational boundary.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Completion confirmation and audit-ready evidence capture
workflow_stages.description: Produce completion records only when action history, final state confirmation, approval references, and required customer-impact artifacts are present and safely handled.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: Fintech servicing or fulfillment work item with product, rail, program, and requested next action
Sponsor-bank or tenant core ledger records, wallet or FBO subledger records, and processor event history
Settlement files, ACH return or reversal records, issuer-processor statuses, and exception queue notes
Customer communication logs, disclosures, notices, servicing notes, and complaint or dispute references
Approval history, escalation notes, program playbooks, and role-owner maps
Documented exception, rework, blocked-state, and next-owner artifacts
Completion evidence record with final state, action trace, approval references, and customer-impact controls
systems_of_record: Sponsor-bank core or deposit servicing platforms
Wallet ledger, FBO-account, or internal subledger platforms
Issuer-processor, payment-orchestration, ACH, or settlement systems
Approval workflow, policy repository, and audit-log systems

allowed: Classify work as in scope, blocked, or out of scope based on fintech operations ownership and the available evidence.
Validate the minimum tenant facts, source records, and authority context required to move the work item to the next lifecycle state.
Advance, hold, rework, or complete the work when the controlling ledger, processor, approval, and communication artifacts support that change.
Prepare execution status updates, exception notes, completion records, and escalation packages tied to named fintech records.
escalate: Sponsor-bank ownership, processor truth, or system-of-record precedence is unclear or contradictory.
Public baseline guidance and tenant or sponsor-bank policy materially conflict on whether work may proceed.
Fraud, AML, sanctions, complaint adjudication, Reg E interpretation, KYC remediation, or legal interpretation is required.
The requested action changes customer funds, disclosures, or account status without the documented approval owner.
refuse: Requests to fabricate completion, approval, processor, ledger, or customer-notice evidence.
Requests to approve or execute fund release, credits, fee waivers, account restrictions, or policy overrides without the documented authority owner.
Requests to treat fintech operations as generic support work without product, rail, program, or source-of-truth context.

domain_failure_modes: Advancing work on a generic ticket narrative without verifying sponsor-bank, ledger, processor, and customer-communication truth.
Treating internal wallet or subledger records as interchangeable with sponsor-bank or processor records when precedence is undefined.
Closing or clearing ACH or processor exceptions without the return, reversal, settlement, or approval artifacts required by the governing workflow.
Letting customer-impacting actions appear operationally routine when they actually require sponsor-bank, risk, compliance, or legal approval.
Flattening sponsor-bank, program-manager, platform-operator, and processor responsibilities into one generic fintech owner.
Producing completion records that omit final state confirmation, approval references, or required customer-notice evidence.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Whether the tenant treats sponsor-bank core records, internal wallet records, or processor records as the controlling final-state source for each product and rail
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant source-of-truth precedence by product and rail at runtime and block high-impact state changes until the controlling record path is explicit.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Which party approves customer-impacting actions in multi-party embedded-finance programs where sponsor-bank, program-manager, and platform roles differ by action type
unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Require tenant approval-owner maps for refunds, funds release, fee waivers, account restrictions, and notice exceptions before completion or override packaging.

notes: Research is intentionally constrained to authoritative U.S. public sources because the issue did not specify a non-U.S. jurisdictional baseline.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains because missing jurisdiction, source-of-truth, and approval-owner facts are converted into explicit escalation and blocked-state rules.
